coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots enhanced pollutant degradation and bioelectricity generation in bioelectrochemical systems
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Gaoqi
Chen, Binghan
Wang, Tianyi
Ning, Wei
Du, Baosheng
Lv, Jialong
Guo, Jiaqi
Yang, Caixuan
Li, Jian
Liu, Shouxin
Chen, Zhijun
Li, Shujun
Yang, Chenhui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), which enhance extracellular electron transfer, have huge potential for boosting microbial metabolism within bioelectrochemical systems (BESs). Despite this, accelerated metabolic degradation of contaminants via CD-mediated enhancement of electron transfer in BESs has rarely been reported. In this study, a nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with electron-rich and graphite-like carbon core structure was synthesized by simple hydrothermal technology using 5-hydroxymethylfurfural and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials and applied to BESs. By combining N-CDs with bacterial cell components, an efficient electron transfer pathway was constructed to promote extracellular and intracellular electron transfer. In addition, the treatment effect of the system on different types of pollutants (such as sulfamethoxazole, phenolic compounds and papermaking wastewater) was also tested. The experimental results showed that N-CDs could significantly improve the metabolic activity of microorganisms in BESs. The degradation rate of sulfamethoxazole, phenols and organic matter in papermaking wastewater was 4.4, 3.4 and 1.9 times that of the blank group, respectively. Concurrently, N-CDs significantly enhanced the bioelectricity production of the system. This work is the first to explore in detail the mechanism of N-CDs as an electronic medium to accelerate pollutant degradation and promote bioelectricity generation in BESs, providing new ideas for the development of efficient and environmentally friendly wastewater treatment technologies and renewable energy production programs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioelectrochemical systems
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Electron transfer
Pollutant biodegradation
Bioelectricity generation

Electrochemical measurements in microbial electrochemical cells and microbial fuel cells
Degradation of sulfamethoxazole and assessment of microbial viability in presence of byproducts
Degradation of phenol and lignocellulosic wastewater
Morphology, structure and composition of CDs
Assessment of biosafety and cellular internalization of CDs
Augmentation of SMX degradation by CDs in BESs
Universality of CD-augmented pollutant degradation in BESs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic showing synthesis of CDs; (b) TEM images of CDs; (c) Raman spectrum of CDs; (d) XPS spectrum of CDs; CLSM images of (e) pristine bacteria and (f) CDs-bacteria; TEM images of (g) pristine bacteria and (h) CDs-bacteria; Cross-sectional TEM images of (i) pristine bacteria and (j) CDs-bacteria.

(a) CV curves of CDs-bacteria and pristine bacteria in MEC; (b) IT curves of CDs-bacteria and pristine bacteria in MEC; (c) EIS impedance spectra of CDs-bacteria and pristine bacteria in MEC; (d) Output voltage curves over three cycles in MFC; (e) Extent of SMX degradation at different time points in MFC; (f) Schematic showing SMX degradation by CDs-bacteria; (g) Degradation pathway of SMX by CDs-bacteria in MFC.

Degradation of phenol (a) IT curve; (b) Output voltage curves of CDs-bacteria and pristine bacteria over three cycles; (c) Extent of phenol degradation at different time points; Degradation of papermaking wastewater (d) IT curve; (e) Output voltage curves of CDs-bacteria and pristine bacteria over three cycles; (f) Extent of COD removal from papermaking wastewater at different time points.
The microbial community analysis at (a) genus level and (b) family level of cow dung flora.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots enhanced pollutant degradation and bioelectricity generation in bioelectrochemical systems

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), which enhance extracellular electron transfer, have huge potential for boosting microbial metabolism within bioelectrochemical systems (BESs). Despite this, accelerated metabolic degradation of contaminants via CD-mediated enhancement of electron transfer in BESs has rarely been reported. In this study, a nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with electron-rich and graphite-like carbon core structure was synthesized by simple hydrothermal technology using 5-hydroxymethylfurfural and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials and applied to BESs. By combining N-CDs with bacterial cell components, an efficient electron transfer pathway was constructed to promote extracellular and intracellular electron transfer. In addition, the treatment effect of the system on different types of pollutants (such as sulfamethoxazole, phenolic compounds and papermaking wastewater) was also tested. The experimental results showed that N-CDs could significantly improve the metabolic activity of microorganisms in BESs. The degradation rate of sulfamethoxazole, phenols and organic matter in papermaking wastewater was 4.4, 3.4 and 1.9 times that of the blank group, respectively. Concurrently, N-CDs significantly enhanced the bioelectricity production of the system. This work is the first to explore in detail the mechanism of N-CDs as an electronic medium to accelerate pollutant degradation and promote bioelectricity generation in BESs, providing new ideas for the development of efficient and environmentally friendly wastewater treatment technologies and renewable energy production programs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Public health is being seriously threatened by the widespread release of contaminants, such as antibiotics and industrial wastewater, into the environment [1–6]. Bioelectrochemical systems (BESs) have enormous potential for the degradation of such contaminants, as well as offering the possibility of simultaneous generation of bioelectricity [7–10]. Electron transfer efficiency is important for intracellular redox reactions and directly affects microbial metabolism [11–14]. In practice, however, non-conductive components within bacterial cells result in poor electron transfer and thus limit the use of BESs in the degradation of contaminants and production of bioenergy [15–17].

Modification of microorganisms by the introduction of conductive nanoparticles, such as Ag and Au has shown great potential for accelerating electron transfer in BESs [18,19]. Carbon dots (CDs), in particular, have been shown to confer excellent electron transfer and photo- and/or electro-catalytic properties because of their conjugated carbon skeleton and surface active functional groups [20–22]. Simple preparation method facilitates multi-functional applications of CDs, which includes hydrothermal, solvothermal, pyrolysis and microwave-assisted method [23]. Previous reports have convincingly demonstrated that CDs increase bioelectricity generation and photosynthesis, this has been attributed to their highly efficient electron transfer and photogenic electron transduction, which augment microbial metabolism [24–29]. Although rapid substrate consumption by the well-established CD-functionalized BESs clearly shows the promise of CDs for improving the efficiency of microbial systems in waste treatment [24,25], there are relatively few reports of CDs boosting the metabolic degradation of contaminants by BESs.

This study aims to explore the potential of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) in enhancing pollutant degradation and bioenergy generation in BESs. Specifically, we used 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (5-HMF) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as raw materials to synthesize nitrogen-doped CDs by a simple hydrothermal technique at 200ºC for 10 h [30], and combined them with BESs to promote the degradation of pollutants in BESs and simultaneous biopower generation. It is mainly used for the degradation of antibiotics, phenolic compounds and papermaking wastewater. By degrading the antibiotic sulfamethoxazole (SMX), we evaluated the mechanism of N-CDs in microbial metabolism, especially how it improves the degradation efficiency of pollutants by providing additional electron transfer pathways. It is found that the enhanced electron transfer capability of CDs is attributed primarily to the predominantly graphitized structure and substantial electron-rich nitrogen doping within the carbon skeleton [31,32]. CDs with specific surface functional groups, such as -OH, and -NH2, show high affinity for bacterial proteins/enzymes because of electrostatic and coordination interactions [33–35]. The unique physicochemical properties of the nitrogen-doped CDs could be used to not only improve electron transfer rates but also to facilitate the breakdown of organic pollutants, thereby augmenting the efficiency of BESs in waste treatment applications. Once integrated into the microbial cell membrane, CDs can act as conductive 'bridges' because of their efficient electron transfer properties. The CDs thereby facilitate the flow of electrons in intracellular metabolic processes and accelerate biocatalysis [36,37]. In addition to assess the versatility of this approach, we extended our investigation to other pollutants, including phenol and lignocellulosic effluent; similar enhancements in degradation rate were seen in both cases.

The main contribution of this study is to reveal the mechanism of nitrogen-doped carbon dots in BESs to improve pollutant degradation and bioelectricity generation by enhancing electron transfer and generating reactive oxygen species, which provides new ideas for the development of efficient environmental pollution control and renewable energy production technology.

The starting materials for preparation of the carbon dots, 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (5-HMF) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD), were purchased from Aladdin Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 50 mmol L−1) was prepared in our laboratory. DL-sodium lactate, the carbon source used as the substrate for cultivation of microbial fuel cell anode biofilms, was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Trading Co. (Shanghai, China). Phenol and SMX, used as typical organic pollutants, were purchased from Aladdin Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Papermaking wastewater was obtained from Puyang Longfeng Paper Co., Ltd., Puyang, Henan Province. The bacteria originated from cow dung flora (Xinxiang City, Henan Province) and were cultured with SMX for three months before the experiments. The Luria-Bertani (LB) broth and LB broth agar medium used for bacterial culture were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The conductive carbon felt used as the electrode was purchased from Tianjin Carbon Manufacturing Company (Tianjin, China).

The composition and structure of the CDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, recorded using a Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the CDs were recorded over the range 10–90° using an X′Pert Pro material powder diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Almelo, Netherlands). Raman spectra, used to characterize the sp2/sp3 hybridization state of the CDs, were recorded using a LabRAM HR Evolution confocal Raman microscope (Horiba, Ltd., Kyoto, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of the CDs were recorded using an ESCALAB 250 Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA. USA). Bacterial morphology was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), using a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The microbial community structure was analyzed by Yanqu Information Technology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China).

5-HMF (0.252 g), OPD (0.108 g) and deionized water (20 mL) were mixed and placed in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave liner. The autoclave was heated to 200ºC for hydrothermal reaction and kept for 10 h. After the hydrothermal reaction is completed and cooled to room temperature, a dark brown CDs solution can be obtained. The mixture is transferred to a 50 mL centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was taken out and filtered using a 0.22 μm aqueous phase filter membrane. Then the filtrate was continuously dialyzed for 7 days using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 200 Da, and deionized water was replaced every 12 h. The dialyzed CDs solution was concentrated by rotary evaporation, and finally the concentrated solution was freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder.

The experimental bacteria were cultivated in microbial fuel cells (MFCs). The original cow dung flora were cultured in the presence of DL-sodium lactate (10 mmol L−1). Over the culture process, the content of DL-sodium lactate in the medium was gradually degreased until it reached 0 mg L−1. Concurrently, the concentration of SMX was gradually increased to 10 mg L−1. Following this cultivation under these conditions, the final obtained microbial community was called as the 'experimental bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of biosafety and cellular internalization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Growth profiles were measured by incubating the bacteria in LB medium at 30ºC in the presence of increasing concentrations of CDs (0–500 μg mL−1), with an oscillation frequency of 160 rpm. The absorbance of the bacteria at 600 nm was then measured using a 96-well plate reader and growth curves were plotted. Staining tests for live/dead cells were also carried out. The conductive carbon felt electrode in the anode chamber was stained at 30ºC for 15 min with a solution of Calcein-AM (2 μmol mL−1) and propidium iodide (4.5 μmol mL−1) in PBS. The electrode was then gently washed three times with PBS and the growth of mixed bacteria on the carbon felt was observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) [38]. To investigate the effect of CDs on the surface and internal morphology of the bacteria, bacterial solutions were incubated in LB medium for 6 h in the presence/absence of CDs. The bacterial solution was then centrifuged at 2500 rpm for 2 min, then was washed by PBS buffer solution 3 times to remove the LB medium. The bacteria were then fixed with PBS containing 2.5 % glutaraldehyde at 4ºC for 12 h. The surface morphology of the bacteria was investigated by TEM; cross sections were used to investigate internal morphology. The conductive carbon felt electrode in the anode chamber was collected and the bacteria on the electrode were fixed with PBS containing 4 % paraformaldehyde at 30ºC for 12 h. The carbon felt was dehydrated by soaking in 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, 95 % and 100 % ethanol solutions for 10 min. The distribution of bacteria on the carbon felt electrode was then observed by SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurements in microbial electrochemical cells and microbial fuel cells
ce:section.ce:para:
For the microbial electrochemical cell (MEC) experiments, a three-electrode electrochemical cell, with a volume of 42 mL, was assembled using acrylic plates. The working electrode was composed of a titanium wire with a diameter of 0.5 mm and carbon cloth with a penetration area of 1 × 2 cm2. The counter electrode was simply a titanium wire of appropriate length, and the reference electrode was a silver/saturated silver chloride electrode. An aliquot (1 mL) of bacterial culture medium (OD
600 = 0.6) was added to the MEC, which was then filled with a solution of SMX in PBS (10 mg L−1). The current curve of the MEC was recorded using a CHI1040C 8 channel potentiostat (CH Instruments, ltd., Shanghai, China) The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve was also recorded over the potential range −0.60 to 0.40 V. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were recorded using a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments Co., ltd., Shanghai, China). For the MFC experiments, a two-chamber MFC was assembled using acrylic plates, with each chamber having a volume of 120 mL. The conductive carbon felt (2 × 4 × 0.5 cm3) used in the MFC was first immersed in acetone for 48 h and then immersed in deionized water for 24 h. The conductive carbon felt was connected with a titanium wire to form the electrode. The anode and cathode were connected by an external resistance of 1000 Ω. The anode and cathode chambers were separated by a CMI-7000S cation exchange membrane (Hangzhou Huamao Technology Co., Ltd., Hangzhou, China). The cathode electrolyte used in the cathode chamber was a solution of K3[Fe(CN)6] in PBS (50 mmoL L−1). The stable MFC anode biofilm was constructed after three cycles of incubation with a solution of SMX in PBS (10 mg L−1). The voltage output of both CD-modified and unmodified MFCs were recorded using a DAQ65410 data acquisition system (Tektronix, Inc., Beaverton, OR, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of sulfamethoxazole and assessment of microbial viability in presence of byproducts
ce:section.ce:para: The anodic liquid of the MFCs was collected at different time points and the bacteria were filtered using a 0.22 μm aqueous phase filter membrane. The degradation pathway of SMX was determined by ultra high-performance liquid chromatography (UPLC). Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were incubated in LB medium, new medium mixed with residues from the degradation of the two experiments, and new medium mixed with 10 mg L−1 sulfamethoxazole for 48 h. The growth curves of the two types of bacterial cells were recorded. At 6 h of incubation, the culture solution of each group of bacterial cells was diluted in a gradient and spread on LB solid medium, and the growth of bacterial cells was evaluated by plate counting method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of phenol and lignocellulosic wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments to measure degradation of phenol and lignocellulosic wastewater were carried out in MFCs. The cathode electrolyte was a solution of K3[Fe(CN)6] (50 mmoL L−1) in PBS and the anode electrolyte was either a solution of phenol (1000 mg L−1) in PBS or a solution of papermaking wastewater in PBS. Samples of the anode solutions of the MFCs were collected at different time points and the bacteria were filtered using a 0.22 μm aqueous phase filter membrane. Uv−Vis spectroscopy was used to detect degradation of phenol and a chemical oxygen demand (COD) test box was used to detect degradation of papermaking wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, structure and composition of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) were produced by hydrothermal reaction of 5-HMF and OPD ( Fig. 1a). Sufficient temperature and time (200ºC for 10 h) offer good N-doping and carbonization of during CDs preparation. The TEM images (Fig. 1b) revealed that the CDs had a normal size distribution, with an average size of ∼3.1 nm (Fig. S1); high-resolution TEM images indicated a lattice spacing close to 0.21 nm, which is comparable to the in-plane lattice parameter of graphene (100) [39]. The XRD pattern (Fig. S2) displayed a broad peak at 21.17°, which is typical of the interlayer spacing found in graphene [40,41]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1 c) showed a distinct disorder-induced D band and a graphitic G band at approximately 1308 cm−1 and 1555 cm−1, respectively. The high IG/ID ratio of around 2.4, suggests a predominance of graphitized structures over disordered carbon within the CDs [42,43].

Further insights into the composition and structure of the CDs were obtained by analysis of the FT-IR and XPS spectra. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. S3) showed a broad peak at 3150 cm−1, indicative of C-OH/N-H stretching vibrations, while the peak at 2840 cm−1 was attributed to C-H tensile vibrations within the aromatic framework. The strong peak at 1710 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibrations of aldehyde CO bonds and aromatic CC bonds. The additional peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 were attributed to CN and C-N stretching vibrations, respectively, and the sharp peaks at 1015 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1 were attributed to C-O-C bending vibrations [44]. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 1d) confirmed the presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in the CDs, with an atomic ratio of 73:18:9 (Fig. S4a). The C 1 s XPS spectrum of the CDs (Fig. S4b) showed peaks at 284.6 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.0 eV, corresponding to CC/C-C, C-N/C-O and CO/CN bonds, respectively [45]. The N 1 s XPS spectrum of the CDs (Fig. S4c) showed binding energy peaks at 399.4 eV, 400.4 eV and 401.6 eV, indicative of pyridinic, pyrrolic and graphitized nitrogen types [46,47]. Lastly, the O 1 s spectrum of the CDs (Fig. S4d) had binding energy peaks at 532.1 eV and 532.9 eV, which are associated with CO and C-O bonds, respectively. These findings confirm that the CDs have surface functional groups containing C, N and O species, such as -OH and -NH2 groups. The level of nitrogen doping in the CDs is relatively high because of the high reactivity between the precursor aldehyde and amine groups. Furthermore, electron-rich nitrogen doping within CDs skeleton is advantageous for electron transfer [48].

It can be seen from the UV absorption spectrum (Fig. S5a) that the main absorption wavelength of CDs is located in the ultraviolet region. On the absorption curve of CDs, the absorption peak at 235 nm is the characteristic peak of the two unsaturated double bond conjugated π-π * transitions of CDs, and the absorption peak at 280 nm is the characteristic peak of the n-π * transition of CO/CN of CDs. This data suggests that CDs synthesized from 5-HMF and OPD precursors possess a significant number of conjugated structures. Furthermore, (Fig. S5b) shows that as the excitation wavelength increases from 340 nm to 390 nm, the fluorescence spectrum of CDs exhibits a slight shift, with fluorescence emission wavelengths ranging from approximately 450 to 475 nm, falling within the blue light spectrum. [49–51]. The fluorescence quantum yield of nitrogen-doped CDs containing 1.81 % can be obtained by calculating the quantum yield of CDs (Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of biosafety and cellular internalization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs demonstrated very favorable biocompatibility with mixed flora. After 140 h coincubation in LB medium, the CDs had little effect on the growth of the cells, even at a concentration of CDs as high as 500 μg mL−1 (Fig. S7a). Viability assays of bacteria on the carbon felt electrodes were conducted, using Calcein-AM to identify living cells and propidium iodide to identify dead cells. CLSM facilitated the examination of green to red fluorescence ratios and thus allowed determination of the live/dead cell distribution in bacteria grown in the presence and absence of CDs (Figs. 1e and 1f). The ratios are very similar (82.3 % vs 79.64 %), indicating that the CDs do not adversely effect the viability of the bacterial cells (Fig. S8).

We next addressed the pivotal question of whether CDs can be used as the sole source of carbon to support bacterial growth. The growth profiles of bacteria in the absence and presence of CDs (100 μg mL−1) were assessed after substituting the LB medium with PBS. There was negligible difference in the growth profiles (Fig. S7b) over a 48 h period. Initial 4 h growth escalation likely reflected the presence of actively dividing cells within the freshly inoculated bacterial suspensions. However, a marked decline in growth trajectories post 44 h pointed to cellular attrition and demonstrated the unsuitability of CDs as a bacterial carbon source. Biofilm formation by bacterial aggregation on carbon felt fibers was shown by SEM to be consistent between CDs-bacteria and pristine bacteria (Fig. S9). The effect of the CDs on bacterial surface morphology was investigated using TEM. The TEM micrographs (Fig. 1h) of the CD-modified bacteria revealed pronounced black particulate deposits on the bacterial surfaces, in stark contrast to the smooth surfaces of pristine bacteria (Fig. 1g). Further cross-sectional TEM analysis showed ingestion of the CDs and their internal distribution within the bacteria. Compared with the pristine bacteria, conspicuous intracellular black aggregates, approximately 15–49 nm in diameter, were seen in the CDs-bacteria (Figs. 1i and 1j), which were significantly larger than native CDs (∼3.1 nm). The TEM micrographs confirm the adsorption and assimilation of the CDs by bacterial cells, with localization on the outer membrane and within the intracellular matrix. This is likely facilitated by the presence of N- and O-containing functional groups, such as -CO, -OH, -CN and -NH2 groups on the surface of the CDs, which can interact with the bacterial cell envelopes and internal structures via electrostatic and/or coordination mechanisms.

The effectiveness of CDs in enhancing the extracellular electron transfer capabilities of bacterial systems was rigorously examined using both microbial electrochemical cells (MECs) and bipartite microbial fuel cells (MFCs), with the antibiotic SMX as the only substrate. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) indicated a superior electrocatalytic response in the CDs-bacteria, with a pronounced amplification of the current density over the range −0.60 to 0.40 V ( Fig. 2a).

This electrocatalytic enhancement was attributed to the inherent ability of the CDs to facilitate electron transfer because of the conjugated structure of their carbon skeleton [52]. Interaction with microbial enzymatic systems then culminates in increased cathodic and anodic peak currents within the CDs-bacteria [25]. Within the MEC configuration, the CDs-bacteria achieved a peak current density of 4.1 μA for almost 2 h, in stark contrast to the pristine bacteria, which achieved a peak of only 0.7 μA (Fig. 2b). The peak current density achieved by the CDs-bacteria was thus 4.9-fold higher than that achieved by pristine bacteria. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) confirmed the superior properties of the CDs-bacteria, which included diminished charge transfer resistance (Rct) [53]. This was evidenced by a contracted EIS Nyquist plot semicircle diameter (Fig. 2c), which, in turn, indicates reduced system impedance and increased electron transfer efficiency. Voltage measurements across a dual-chambered MFC fitted with a unifying external resistor of 1.0 KΩ showed that the CDs-bacteria attained a maximal potential of approximately 0.22 V within 4 h, whereas the pristine bacteria needed ∼60 h to reach a peak potential of only 0.16 V (Fig. 2d). These experiments were carried out in the absence of additional bacterial inoculation, and relied exclusively on pre-established bacterial biofilms on the conductive carbon felt anode. Similarly, in order to more comprehensively evaluate the power generation enhancement effect of the use of CDs on the MFCs system, the power density and polarization curves of the Blank group MFCs system and the CDs group MFCs system were tested and plotted by replacing the resistance. As can be seen from (Fig. S10a), the maximum power density of the Blank group is 2.4 mW/m2, and as can be seen from (Fig. S10b), the maximum power density of the CDs group is 24.1 mW/m2. The maximum power density of the CDs group was 10 times that of the Blank group. The above shows that the use of CDs in MFCs system can improve the power generation effect. As a demonstration of a real-life application of bioelectricity, a mini LED assembly was lit by a CD-modified MFC (Fig. S11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Augmentation of SMX degradation by CDs in BESs
ce:section.ce:para: The enhanced efficacy of MECs and MFCs following incorporation of CDs suggests augmented electron transfer mechanisms within BESs. The biofilm mediated the metabolism and subsequent oxidation of the antibiotic SMX within the anodic compartment, with the consequent electron flux correlating with the generated voltage [54]. The empirical evidence not only suggests that the CDs-bacteria more rapidly achieve elevated current and voltage outputs but also implies accelerated biodegradation of organic contaminants compared with pristine bacteria (Fig. 2e and Fig. S12). UPLC was used to elucidate the kinetic profile of SMX degradation within the MFC anodic chamber at various time points within an operational cycle. CDs-bacteria degraded 80 % of SMX within 144 h, while the pristine bacteria and CDs alone degraded 18 % and 6 % of SMX, respectively, within 144 h (Fig. 2e and Fig. S13). The CDs thus provide a 3.4-fold enhancement in degradation efficiency. Comparative analyses of residual concentrations and biodegradation efficiencies over different time periods revealed superior outcomes in systems with CDs-bacteria. The higher biodegradation and lower residual levels of SMX in the MFC containing CDs-bacteria were attributed to increased electron transfer and thus increased metabolic activity (Fig. 2f). Ultra-high resolution mass spectrometry was used to detect SMX catabolic residues (Fig. 2g). The proposed degradation pathway begins with the oxidation of the -NH2 group on the SMX benzene ring to -NO2 to give N-(5-methylisoxazol-3-yl)-4-nitrobenzenesulfonamide. This is followed by hydrolytic cleavage of the sulfonamide bond, generating 5-methylisoxazol-3-amine and 4-nitrobenzenesulfonic acid, which is further converted into 4-aminobenzenesulfinic acid. Additionally, oxidation of the isoxazole moiety introduces an -OH group, forming 4-amino-N-(4-hydroxy-5-methylisoxazol-3-yl) benzene sulfonamide [55,56].

Considering the widespread use of SMX in clinical settings, degradation efficiency was further assessed by examining the cytotoxicity of the degradation products against model Gram-negative (Escherichia coli) and Gram-positive (Staphylococcus aureus) bacteria. Growth curves of Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus over a 48 h period were compared using four growth media: pure LB medium, LB medium supplemented with degradation residues from CDs-bacteria, LB medium supplemented with degradation residues from pristine bacteria and LB medium supplemented with SMX. The growth curve of E. coli (Fig. S14a) showed the highest bacterial proliferation in pure LB medium, followed by LB medium supplemented with degradation residues from CD-modified bacteria, followed by LB medium supplemented with degradation residues from pristine bacteria; the lowest growth was observed in the presence of SMX (10 mg mL−1). An analogous trend was seen in the growth curves of S. aureus (Fig. S14b). Subsequent agar plating and incubation at 37ºC for 24 h demonstrated that the number of colony-forming units (CFUs) of E. coli decreased in the order LB medium > LB medium supplemented with degradation residues from CDs-bacteria > LB medium supplemented with degradation residues from pristine bacteria > LB medium supplemented with SMX (Fig. S15). These results confirm that SMX biodegradation is higher and the toxicity of the resulting byproducts is lower with CDs-bacteria rather than by pristine bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Universality of CD-augmented pollutant degradation in BESs
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the broad-spectrum efficacy of this protocol for pollutant degradation, we selected pollutants that are prevalent in the petrochemical and organic chemical synthesis sectors. We chose phenol, the prototypical pollutant for experimental degradation studies, and lignocellulosic wastewater, a typical effluent from pulp and paper mills [57,58].

In MECs for phenol degradation, CDs-bacteria demonstrated a significant electrocatalytic response, as shown in the CV curves (Fig. S16a), and a reduced charge transfer resistance, evidenced by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) curves (Fig. S16b). The peak current achieved by the CDs-bacteria was substantially higher than that of pristine bacteria (∼1.7 μA vs. 0.2 μA, Fig. 3a). Electrochemical data also revealed that the maximal voltage achieved in the MFC setup for phenol degradation using CDs-bacteria was significantly greater than that achieved with pristine bacteria (∼0.45 V vs. 0.05 V, Fig. 3b). After 11 days, the phenol concentration in the anodic chamber of the MFC with CDs-bacteria decreased from an initial value of 1000 mg L−1 to ∼124.17 mg L−1 (Fig. 3c and S16c), compared to a markedly higher residual concentration of ∼745.85 mg L−1 in the chamber of the MFC with pristine bacteria. At the same time, it can be seen from the experiment of phenol degradation by CDs alone that the degradation rate within 144 h was only 3 %. It shows that CDs alone cannot directly promote the degradation of phenol (Fig. S17). The degradation pathway of phenol is illustrated in (Fig. S18). Initially, phenol undergoes oxidation to form hydroquinone, which subsequently generates catechol. Catechol, containing two hydroxyl groups, is further oxidized to yield cis,cis-muconic acid. This intermediate is then oxidized to produce β-ketoadipic acid, which undergoes additional oxidation to form succinic acid [59].

Similarly, for lignocellulosic effluent degradation in MECs, CDs-bacteria showed a significant electrocatalytic response (Fig. S19a) and lower charge transfer resistance (Fig. S19b). The peak current achieved by CDs-bacteria was higher than that of pristine bacteria (∼1.7 μA vs. 0.7 μA, Fig. 3d). The peak voltage in the MFC system using CDs-bacteria for lignocellulosic effluent degradation was also significantly higher than that achieved using pristine bacteria (0.71 V vs. 0.10 V, Fig. 3e). The MFC containing CDs-bacteria was more effective at reducing COD in lignocellulosic wastewater; the initial COD level was set at 486.97 mg L−1, and after a 14-day degradation cycle, the residual COD level in the anodic compartment of the MFC with CDs-bacteria was ∼52.19 mg L−1, compared with ∼255.68 mg L−1 in the MFC with pristine bacteria (Fig. 3f and S19c).

These empirical findings confirm that the incorporation of CDs within BESs significantly expedites the degradation of phenol and lignocellulosic effluent pollutants, demonstrating the universal applicability of CDs augmentation for enhancing contaminant degradation and bioelectricity generation in such systems.

To validate the efficacy of utilizing cow dung flora as a degrading microorganism, we conducted an extensive series of microbial diversity experiments at both the genus ( Fig. 4a) and family levels (Fig. 4b). The analysis focused on the most prevalent genera and families, each with an abundance exceeding 10 %. Alcaligenes sp., a genus within the Betaproteobacteria class, is typically Gram-negative, rod-shaped, and facultative anaerobic. This genus can metabolize a wide array of organic compounds, including persistent pollutants such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, dyes, and pesticides. Consequently, Alcaligenes sp. is particularly effective in the remediation of industrial wastewater containing refractory organic substances [60]. A similar trend was evident within the Alcaligenaceae family (Fig. 4b). Providencia sp., recognized for its natural drug resistance and facultative anaerobic nature, was detected in the cow dung flora. This genus’ metabolic versatility and pollutant-degradation proficiency underline its significant potential for environmental remediation, especially for contaminants like petroleum hydrocarbons and pesticides [61]. Another pivotal genus identified was Stenotrophomonas sp., part of the Gammaproteobacteria class. These are Gram-negative, rod-shaped, facultative anaerobes capable of exploiting a variety of carbon and nitrogen sources, encompassing complex organic compounds and inorganic substances [62]. Clostridium sp., a genus in the Firmicutes phylum, is widely distributed in environments such as soil, water, and animal intestines. Some Clostridium species can degrade recalcitrant organic pollutants like aromatic compounds and chlorinated hydrocarbons [63]. A similar distribution pattern was observed within the Clostridiaceae family (Fig. 4b).

At the family level, the communities Morganellaceae and Xanthnomonadaceae, both affiliates of the Gammaproteobacteria class, were noted. Predominantly Gram-negative and rod-shaped, these facultative anaerobes are adept at utilizing various organic compounds as carbon and energy sources, facilitating the treatment of industrial wastewater laden with recalcitrant organics, thereby mitigating toxic and harmful substances [64]. The microbial diversity analysis data substantiates that the selected cow dung flora encompasses a substantial concentration of functional bacteria with degradative capabilities, deeming it suitable for broad-spectrum applications in antibiotic degradation.

In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method using 5-hydroxymethylfurfural and o-phenylenediamine, and applied to bacterial enhancement systems (BESs). The results showed that these CDs could significantly improve the oxidative degradation efficiency of organic pollutants (such as sulfamethoxazole, phenol and pollutants in lignocellulosic wastewater), which were 4.4, 3.4 and 1.9 times that of the blank group, respectively, and promoted the generation of bioelectricity. Nitrogen-doped CDs have good biocompatibility. Its high degree of graphitization and crystallinity as well as electron-rich nitrogen doping contribute to electron migration. After binding to bacterial proteins, it amplifies the electrochemical activity of microorganisms, thereby increasing the electron transfer rate, reducing the electrochemical impedance, and increasing the yield of bioelectricity. The accelerated and improved electron transfer promoted the metabolism of bacteria, thereby enhancing the oxidative degradation of sulfamethoxazole and reducing the biological toxicity of degradation by-products. CDs showed a broad-spectrum effect on the enhancement of the ability of BESs to degrade other organic pollutants (i.e., phenol and lignocellulose wastewater).

By incorporating nitrogen-doped CDs, this research expands the scope of BESs and provides new insights and theoretical support for the use of polyhedral nitrogen-doped CDs in environmental remediation and energy production. Through an in-depth analysis of the biological safety and ecological impact of N-CDs, this study provide the potential of the integration of CDs with other materials or technologies, further broadening their potential for multifunctional applications in BESs. Future research is poised to unlock the full potential of N-CDs, driving technological innovation and advancements in related BESs fields.

Gaoqi Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Binghan Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Tianyi Wang: Writing – review & editing. Wei Ning: Writing – review & editing. Baosheng Du: Writing – review & editing. Jialong Lv: Writing – review & editing. Jiaqi Guo: Writing – review & editing. Caixuan Yang: Writing – review & editing. Jian Li: Writing – review & editing. Shouxin Liu: Writing – review & editing. Zhijun Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shujun Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Chenhui Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Research and Development Plan of Heilongjiang Province (CN) (GZ20220115), National Natural Science Foundation of China (32171716, 31890774), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M690572), the Heilongjiang Provincial Postdoctoral Science Foundation (LBHZ21033) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572022BB05).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.115451.
